summary: I started working in a hosiery mill in Winston-Salem when I was just 14 years old. My sister and I both worked there, and she has stayed at that same mill for over 51 years. We used to walk to work every day, and it was in Winston-Salem that I met John, who later became my husband. We lived near there before moving to the country to help on John's mother's farm.

My son Van had scarlet fever and was very sick for a long time, nearly two years after he got it. It was a frightening time for our family, and it affected our plans to build and improve our home. Scarlet fever was one of the many illnesses that challenged us as our family grew.

I remember the flu epidemic back in 1918, the Spanish flu pandemic. It was a terrible time; everyone was so afraid they wouldn’t come near us and would leave food on the porch. I caught the flu when my baby Virgil was only three days old, and John had it worse than the rest of us. That illness left me weak for a long time afterward.

When my son Ernest got badly hurt from a gunshot wound, we had to take him to the hospital in Elkin. I stayed there with him while the doctors tried to find the bullet through X-rays and surgery, but they never could get it out. That hospital stay in Elkin was costly and left us in debt for a long time.

During a hard winter when we had no money and couldn't feed the children properly, I went down to Yadkinville to ask for help from the Relief people. They were very kind and provided us with food and necessities when we were struggling the most. That assistance was a blessing during those difficult times.

Virginia was mentioned as a place where my son Van served after he left home. He stayed there until their money ran out and then came back with an honorable discharge. It was part of his journey growing up and learning responsibility.

New Jersey was where my son Clarence served in the army for 11 months. It was a time when he was away from home, and we missed him dearly. His service was part of the family's contribution during those years.

John was a faithful man who prayed every morning before going to work and attended the Baptist church regularly. Our funeral for John was held at the Baptist church, where many friends gathered, and the choir sang hymns. The church was an important part of our community and family life.

Alongside the Baptist church, John also attended the Pentecostal Holiness church every Sunday. It was part of his spiritual routine and faith. I still go to these churches myself, and they have been a source of comfort and fellowship for our family.
